ڈیرہ.e: Inherited from Proto-Indo-Aryan *ḍēra. Compare Punjabi ڈیرَہ (ḍerah) / ਡੇਰਾ (ḍerā), Nepali डेरा (ḍerā), Sindhi دڙو (dṛo) / दड़ो (daṛo), ڈھورو / ढोरो (ḍhoro), Gujarati ડેરો (ḍero), ધોરો (dhoro), Marathi डेरा (ḍerā), Assamese ডেৰা (dera).

senses.g: Dera Ismail Khan (a city in Dera Ismail Khan district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

ڈیرہ اسماعیل خان.e: From Urdu ڈیرہ (“tent”) and named after Baloch mercenary Ismail Khan.

senses.g: Dera Allah Yar (a city in Jafarabad district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ڈیرہ اللہ یار.e: From Urdu ڈیرہ (“tent”) and named after assassinated political leader Mir Allahyar Khan Khosa.

senses.g: Dera Bugti (a city in Dera Bugti district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ڈیرہ بگٹی.e: From Urdu ڈیرہ (“tent”) and named after the Bugti tribe.

senses.g: alternative form of دِہْرَہ دُون (dihra dūn)
Dera Ghazi Khan (a city in Dera Ghazi Khan district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ڈیرہ غازی خان.e: From Urdu ڈیرہ (“tent”) and named after Ghazi Khan.

senses.g: Dera Murad Jamali (a city in Nasirabad district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ڈیرہ مراد جمالی.e: From Urdu ڈیرہ (“tent”) and named after Murad Jamali.

ڈیڑھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀺𑀯𑀟𑁆𑀠 (divaḍḍha), from Sanskrit द्व्यर्ध (dvyardha, “one and a half”).

ڈیوڑھی.e: Inherited from Sanskrit देहुडी (dehuḍī). Compare Punjabi ڈیوڈھی (ḍiyoḍhī) / ਡਿਉਢੀ (ḍiuḍhī).

ex.e: organizational structure
two-hundred and fifty thousand rupees

ڈھائی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi अढाई (aḍhāī), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀟𑁆𑀠𑀸𑀇𑀬 (aḍḍhāiya), from Sanskrit अर्धतृतीय (ardhatṛtīya, “[two full ones and] the half of the third”). Cognate with Assamese আঢ়ৈ (arhoi), Bengali আড়াই (aṛai), Gujarati અઢી (aḍhī).

senses.g: bottle cap or a cork to close a bottle
dholak, a type of North Indian hand drum

ڈھونڈنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀠𑀼𑀁𑀠𑀼𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀤𑀺 (ḍhuṃḍhulladi), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀥𑀼𑀁𑀠𑁆 (*dhuṃḍh). Compare Hindi ढूँढ़ना (ḍhū̃ṛhnā) / ढूँढना (ḍhūṇḍhnā) and Punjabi ڈُھونْڈݨا (ḍhūnḍṇā) / ਢੂੰਡਣਾ (ḍhūṇḍṇā).

ڈھیر.e: Inherited from Proto-Middle Indo-Aryan *𑀠𑁂𑀭 (*ḍhera). Compare Punjabi ڈھیر (ḍher, “pile”), Gujarati ઢેર (ḍher, “pile”), Marathi ढेर (ḍher, “pile”), Bengali ঢেরি (ḍheri, “heap, pile”).

senses.g: bray, braying (hee-haw, the sound of a donkey)

ڈھيٹ.e: Inherited from Sanskrit धृष्ट (dhṛṣṭa). Compare Punjabi ڈِھیْٹھ (ḍhiīṭh) / ਢੀਠ (ḍhīṭh).

senses.g: The thirteenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ڈ and followed by ر.

ذات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذات (zāt), from Arabic ذَات (ḏāt).

ذاتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذَاتِی (zātī), borrowed from Arabic ذَاتِيّ (ḏātiyy), from ذَات (ḏāt, “self”) + ـِيّ (-iyy).

ذائقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذَائِقَه (zā'iqa), from Arabic ذَائِقَة (ḏāʔiqa). Related to ذوق (zauq).

ذبح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذِبْح (zibh), from Arabic ذَبْح (ḏabḥ).

ذرا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذره (zarra), from Arabic ذَرَّة (ḏarra).

senses.g: kindly, please, would you?, could you?, may I?, if you will, if you may, if you please

ex.e: Close the door, if you may

ذرا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذره (zarra), from Arabic ذَرَّة (ḏarra).

ذرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذره (zarra), from Arabic ذَرَّة (ḏarra).

ذریعہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذَرِیعَه (zarī'a), from Arabic ذَرِيعَة (ḏarīʕa, “pretext”).

ذکر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذکر (zakar), from Arabic ذَكَر (ḏakar).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ذکر (zikr), from Arabic ذِكْر (ḏikr).

senses.g: remembrance of God (often in one's heart)

ذکر الہی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذِکْرِ اِلٰهِی (zikr-i ilāhī), a calque of Arabic ذِكْرِ الله (ḏikri llāh). By surface analysis, ذِکْر (zikr, “mention; remembrance”) + اِلٰہی (ilāhī, “divine”).

ذلت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذِلَّت (zillat), from Arabic ذِلَّة (ḏilla).

ذلیل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذلیل (zalīl), from Arabic ذَلِيل (ḏalīl). Compare Punjabi ذليل / ਜ਼ਲੀਲ (zalīl).

senses.g: Dhu'l-Hijja (twelfth and final month of the Islamic calendar, during which the hajj takes place)

ذو الحجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذوالحجه (zūlhijja), from Arabic ذُو الحِجَّة (ḏū l-ḥijja). By surface analysis, Arabic ذُو (ḏū, “possessed of”) + Arabic الحِجَّة (al-ḥijja, “the hajj”).

senses.g: Dhu'l-Kifl (a prophet of Islam, sometimes identified with Ezekiel in the Bible)
a male given name from Arabic

ذو الکفل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذُوالْکِفْل (zū-l-kifl), from Arabic ذُو الْكِفْل (ḏū l-kifl).

ex.e: To have a taste in urdu

ذوق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذوق (zawq), from Arabic ذَوْق (ḏawq). Related to ذائقہ (zāyqā).

ذہن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذِهْن (zihn), from Arabic ذِهْن (ḏihn).

ذہنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذهنی (zihnī, zahanī), from Arabic ذِهْنِيّ (ḏihniyy).

senses.g: having a retentive mind; having good memory

ذہین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ذهین (zahīn), from Arabic ذَهِين (ḏahīn).

senses.g: The fourteenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ذ and followed by ڑ.

ex.u: ریڈیو پر ان سے رابطہ نہیں ہو پا رہا۔
ex.r: reḍīo par un se rābt̤ah nahīṉ ho pā rahā.
ex.e: I'm not able to get in-touch with them on the radio

رابطہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَابِطَه (rābita), Arabic رَابِطَة (rābiṭa).

ex.e: The moon comes out at night.
Thursday

senses.g: time (a period of time; one that is finite)

ex.u:
بن جو کچھ بن سکے جوانی میں
رات تو تھوڑی ہے بہت ہے سانگ
ex.r:
ban jo kuch ban sake javānī mẽ
rāt to thoṛī hai bahut hai sāṅg
ex.e:
Acquire whatever you can in the season of youth
The task is arduous and the time short

رات.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi رات (rat /⁠rāt⁠/) / رت (rt) / رتی (rty /⁠ratī⁠⁠/), from Old Hindi رت (rat) / رتی (/⁠ratī⁠/), inherited from Prakrit 𑀭𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺 (ratti), from Sanskrit रात्रि (rā́trī).

senses.g: used to laugh off a forgotten promise (ie. "that was yesterday, today is today")

رات گئی بات گئی.e: Literally, “The night has passed away [gone], (as has) the matter passed away [gone]”.

ex.e: British rule in Hindustan/India

senses.g: A prefix which forms a large number of compound words and carries an adjectival meaning of the king or of the state (e.g., راجکر (rāj-kar) royal tribute, tax, راجدوت (rāj-dūt) royal emissary, ambassador, راجبھاشا (rāj-bhāṣā) national language, راجنیتی (rāj-nīti) politics), etc.
A suffix which forms compound words and carries the meaning king or chief (e.g., مہاراج (mahā-rāj) great king, maharaja).

راج شاہی.e: Coined after تاناشاہی (tānāśāhī), from راج (rāj, “royalty, reign”) + شاہی (śāhī, “rule, -cracy”).

راجا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit राजा (rājā), nominative singular of राजन् (rājan).

راجپوت.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit राजपुत्र (rājaputra, “prince”). By surface analysis, راج (rāj, “king”) + پُوت (pūt, “son”). Doublet of راوت (rāvat).
Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit राजपुत्र (rājaputra, “prince”). By surface analysis, راج (rāj, “king”) + پُوت (pūt, “son”). Doublet of راوت (rāvat).
Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit राजपुत्र (rājaputra, “prince”). By surface analysis, راج (rāj, “king”) + پُوت (pūt, “son”). Doublet of راوت (rāvat).

senses.g: head of state's residence, capital city
king's residence, a palace, royal city, seat of governor, capital, metropolis
Rajasthan (a state in northwestern India, created in 1949 and replacing the former province of Rajputana)
Rajasthani (of, from or relating to Rajasthan)

راجن پور.e: Named after Makhdoom Sheikh Rajan Shah, from راجَن (rājan) + ـپُور (-pūr).

senses.g: Rajanya, the ancient Kshatriya of the Iron Age
Rajouri, Rajauri (a city, a district of Jammu and Kashmir, India)
Rajya Sabha (the upper house of the Parliament of India)

راحت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَاحَت (rāhat), borrowed from Arabic رَاحَة (rāḥa), from رَاحَ (rāḥa). First attested in c. 1586 as Middle Hindi راحت (raḥt /⁠rāhat⁠/).

راز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَاز (rāz). First attested in c. 1636 as Middle Hindi راز (raz).
Borrowed from Classical Persian رَاز (rāz). First attested in c. 1636 as Middle Hindi راز (raz).

راستہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian راسته (rāsta). Compare Bengali রাস্তা (rasta), Gujarati રસ્તો (rasto), Kannada ರಸ್ತೆ (raste), Marathi रस्ता (rastā), Punjabi ਰਸਤਾ (rastā).

راشٹر.e: From Sanskrit राष्ट्र (rāṣṭra). Doublet of راٹھ (rāṭh), a tadbhava; see also مَراٹھی (marāṭhī).

راضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian راضی, from Arabic رَاضٍ (rāḍin).

راکھ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi راکھ (rakh) (c. 1635), from Prakrit 𑀭𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀸 (rakkhā), from Sanskrit रक्षा (rakṣā).

راکھس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀭𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀲 (rakkhasa), from Sanskrit राक्षस (rākṣasa). Doublet of راکھشس (rākśas), a tatsama.

senses.g: a demon, a malignant deity at war with the gods in Hindu mythology; a rakshasa

راکھشس.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit राक्षस (rākṣasa). Doublet of راکَھس (rākhas), a tadbhava.

senses.g: thread tied to a brother's hand by his sister.

ex.e: God knows

senses.g: a male given name, Ram or Rāma, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

رام.e: From Sanskrit राम (rāma). Cognate to Bengali রাম (ram), Punjabi رام (rām)/ਰਾਮ (rām), Marathi राम (rām), Nepali राम (rām).

رام اللہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic رَامَ اللّٰه (rāma llāh).

رامائن.e: Learned borrowing from Classical Sanskrit रामायण (rāmāyaṇa).

senses.g: a surname, Ranjha, from Punjabi — Ranjha (the hero of the folk tragedy Heer Ranjha.)
the First Lady of a state, empire

رانی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀭𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀻 (raṇṇī), from Sanskrit राज्ञी (rā́jñī, “queen”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₃rḗǵnih₂ (“queen”).

senses.g: Rawalpindi (a city in Rawalpindi district, Punjab, Pakistan)

راولپنڈی.e: From Punjabi راولپنڈی (rāvalpinḍī), named after Bappa Rawal.

راہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَاه (rāh). First attested in c. 1505 as Middle Hindi راہ (rah /⁠rāh⁠/).

راہب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian راهب (rāhib), from Arabic رَاهِب (rāhib). Compare Punjabi راہب / ਰਾਹਬ (rāhab).

راہبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَاهِبَه (rāhiba), from Arabic رَاهِبَة (rāhiba).

senses.g: passage (ie. corridor, an allowed or safe route for travel)

ex.u: پاکستان کے وزیرِ اعظم نے گرودوارہ دربار صاحب کرتار پور کی نئے سرے سے تعمیر اور انڈین یاتریوں کے لیے تیار کی گئی راہداری کا افتتاح کر دیا ہے۔
ex.r: pākistān ke vazīr-e aʻz̤am ne gurodvāra darbār ṣāḥib kartār pūr kī na'ye sire se taʻmīr aur inḍiyan yatriyoṉ ke li'ye tayyār kī ga'ī rāhdārī kā iftitāḥ kar diya hai.
ex.e: The Prime Minister of Pakistan has inaugurated the newly constructed Gurudwara Darbar Sahib Kartarpur and the corridor prepared for Indian pilgrims.

senses.g: toll, road tax (usually in conjunction with محصول (maḥṣūl, “tax”))

ex.u: وہ بھی نووارد مسافر تھے ایک کے پاس کاغذ راہداری تھا اسے حکم ہوا کہ روانہ ہو جاؤ۔
ex.e: They, too, were newcomers, one had a travel document and was instructed to leave [forthwith].

راہداری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian راهداری (rāhdārī, “toll road”). By surface analysis, راہدار (rāhdār, “toll-gatherer, toller”) + ی (ī). Compare Nepali राहदानी (rāhadānī).

senses.g: the title of officials and dignitaries, as well as the kings and royal families of former India

رائے.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀭𑀸𑀬 (rāya), from Sanskrit राजा (rā́jā, “king”). Doublet of رَاجَا (rājā).
Borrowed from Classical Persian رَای (rāy) / رأی (ra'y), from Arabic رَأْي (raʔy).

رب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رب (rab, rabb), from Arabic رَبّ (rabb).

senses.g: clipping of ہائے رَبّا (hāe rabbā, “oh Lord!”)

ربا.e: Borrowed from Punjabi رَبّا (rabbā), from رَبّ (rabb, “Lord”).

رباعی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رباعی (rubā'ī), from Arabic رُبَاعِيّ (rubāʕiyy), related to أَرْبَعَة (ʔarbaʕa, “four”).

senses.g: The piece of land where the Dome of the Rock is sited in Jerusalem, called so because it is higher than its surroundings.
Rabwah (a city in Chiniot district, Punjab, Pakistan), an important city for Ahmadi Muslims and former headquarters of the Ahmadiyya community, an area higher than its surroundings.
Rabi I (third month of the Islamic calendar and birth month of the prophet Muhammad)

ربیع الاول.e: Borrowed from Arabic رَبِيعُ الأَوَّل (rabīʕu l-ʔawwal). By surface analysis, Arabic رَبِيع (rabīʕ, “spring, Rabi”) + Arabic الأَوَّل (al-ʔawwal, “first”).

رتبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رتبه (rutba), from Arabic رُتْبَة (rutba).

رتو.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit ऋतु (ṛtú). Doublet of رت (rut).

ex.e: Rath Yatra

رجعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَجْعَت (raj'at), from Arabic رِجْعَة (rijʕa).

senses.g: may God have mercy on him

رحمۃ اللہ علیہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic رَحْمَةُ اللَّٰهِ عَلَيْه (raḥmatu llāhi ʕalayh).

رحیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَحِیم (rahīm), from Arabic رَحِيم (raḥīm).

senses.g: Rahim Yar Khan (a city in Rahim Yar Khan district, Punjab, Pakistan)

رحیم یار خان.e: Named after Sadeq Mohammad Khan IV.

ex.e: to change countenance; to become angry or displeased
to turn away; to change the direction of one's face; to reject; to be inattentive

senses.g: a female given name, Rukhsar, from Persian
leave, permission to leave, dismission, departure

رخصت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُخْصَت (ruxsat), from Arabic رُخْصَة (ruḵṣa).

senses.g: on leave; away; one who is away on leave or on furlough
money or present given to someone prior to their departure
a wedding ceremony in which the bride is sent away to her husband's home, as the final ceremony

رد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رد (radd), from Arabic رَدّ (radd).

رزق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رِزْق (rizq), from Arabic رِزْق (rizq), from Middle Persian 𐭫𐭥𐭰𐭩𐭪 (rōzīg). Doublet of روزِی (rōzī).

رژیم.e: Borrowed from Persian رژیم (režim), from French régime.

senses.g: the best or prime part (of anything), essence, marrow, pith
constituent fluid or essential bodily fluid

رس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀭𑀲 (rasa), from Sanskrit रस (rása).

رسالہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رساله (risāla), from Arabic رِسَالَة (risāla).

senses.g: to bring out, publish (a magazine, treatise, periodical, etc.)

رستم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُسْتَم (rustam), from Middle Persian lwtsthm (/⁠rōdstahm⁠/), from lwd (/⁠rōd⁠/, “river”) + stʾhmb (/⁠stahm⁠/, “force, power”).

ex.e: to perform the last rites

رسم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رسم (rasm), from Arabic رَسْم (rasm).

رسم الخط.e: From Arabic رَسْم (rasm, “pattern, design”) + Arabic الخَط (al-ḵaṭ, “handwriting”).

رسمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رسمی (rasmī), from Arabic رَسْمِيّ (rasmiyy). Compare Punjabi رسمی / ਰਸਮੀ (rasmī).

رسن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَسَن (rasan, “rope; string”). Doublet of رَشْمِی (raśmī) and رَشْنا (raśnā) (borrowings from Sanskrit र॒श्मि (raśmí) and र॒श॒ना (raśanā́) respectively).

ex.e: Prophet of God (PBUH)
John the Apostle

رسول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَسُول (rasūl), from Arabic رَسُول (rasūl). First attested in c. 1500 as Middle Hindi رسول (rsvl).

رسی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀭𑀲𑁆𑀲𑀺 (rassi), from Sanskrit रश्मि (raśmi), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *raćmíš.

ex.u: تین لاکھ روپیہ کا جواہر ... اسی وقت دے کر رسید دستخطی لے لو
ex.e: You can buy the gems ... worth three hundred thousand rupees this instance along with the signed receipt

ex.u: شہزادہ کی جہاں کہیں جانے کی مرضی ہو باحتیاط تمام وہاں پہنچا دے ان کی رسید مجھے لا دے
ex.e: wherever the prince wishes to go, take him that there with care [and] bring me the confirmation [of his safe travel]

ex.u: بیٹی جا بیگموں کو سلام کر، ہنس بول ، پر گُم سُم بیٹھی ہے اور رسید ہی نہیں
ex.e: Girl, go convey my greetings to [my] wives, laugh and talk, but she's sat all gloomy and [came] no response

رسید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رسید (rasīd, “receipt”, literally “it has arrived”). False cognate of English receipt.

رشتہ دار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رشته دار (rišta-dār), from رشته (rišta) + دار (dār). Compare Punjabi ਰਿਸ਼ਤੇਦਾਰ (riśtedār).

رشوت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رِشْوَه (rišwa), from Arabic رِشْوَة (rišwa). First attested in c. 1654 as Middle Hindi رشوت (rśvt).

رشی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit ऋषि (ṛṣi). Inherited from Middle Hindi رشی (rśy /⁠riśī⁠/) (c. 1672).

senses.g: a male given name, Rishi, of Hindu usage

رشی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit ऋषि (ṛṣi). Inherited from Middle Hindi رشی (rśy /⁠riśī⁠/) (c. 1672).

رضا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رضا (rizā), from Arabic رِضَا (riḍā).

senses.g: a volunteer (specifically one who takes part in humanitarian efforts)
a Pakistani razakar who aided the Pakistani army against the Bangladeshi Mukti Bahini
a Hyderabadi razakar who fought against the Indian integration of Hyderabad State

رضا کار.e: From رَضا (razā, “will, pleasure, contentness”) + کار (kār, “doer”), literally “the one who seeks [the] will [of God]”. The term رَضا کَار (razā kār) implies one who carries out an act of kindness for the sake of obtaining the will and pleasure of God above anything else. See related terms رَضائے اِلٰہی (razāe ilāhī, “divine consent”) and رَضائے خُدا (razāe xudā, “the will of God”).

senses.g: oblique plural of رَضا کَار (razā kār, “volunteer”)

رطل.e: From Arabic رِطْل (riṭl), from Aramaic רִיטְלָא (rīṭlā), earlier לִיטְרָא (līṭrā), from Ancient Greek λίτρα (lítra).

رعایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رعایت (ri'āyat), from Arabic رِعَايَة (riʕāya).

رفتہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رفته (rafta), past participle of رفتن (raftan).

رفتہ رفتہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رفته رفته (rafta-rafta).

رفقا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُفَقا (rufaqā), from Arabic رُفَقَاء (rufaqāʔ).

senses.g: one who watches over (guardian, guard; watchman, waiter of something)

رقیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَقِیب (raqīb), borrowed from Arabic رَقِيب (raqīb), from رَقَبَ (raqaba). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi رَقِیب (rqyb /⁠raqīb⁠/).

senses.g: an annual rite, or ceremony, in which a rakhi (thread) is tied to a brother's hand by his sister in exchange for a gift to her
rak'a, one of two to four cycles of Islamic prayer (salat)

رکعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رکعت (rak'at), ultimately from Arabic رَكْعَة (rakʕa).

senses.g: Rigveda; Vedas, ancient hymns of the historical Vedic religion written in Vedic Sanskrit, taking place and composed in ancient Punjab and Khyber.

رگوید.e: Perhaps a medieval borrowing directly from Sanskrit ऋग्वेद (ṛgvedá) or a loanword via Classical Persian from the same source. Compare Ottoman Turkish وید (vid, “Vedas”).

senses.g: of or pertaining to a symbol or symbols

رمزی.e: Borrowed from Arabic رَمْزِيّ (ramziyy). By surface analysis, رمز (ramz, “symbol”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: Ramadan (the holy ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar, during which Muslims fast between the break of dawn until sunset; they also refrain from drinking liquids, smoking and having sexual relations)
a male given name, Ramzan or Ramazan, from Arabic

رمضان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَمَضَان (ramazān), from Arabic رَمَضَان (ramaḍān).

رمہ.e: From Classical Persian رمه (rama, “herd, flock; troop”).

رنڈی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀭𑀡𑁆𑀟 (raṇḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓- (-ika-) without cluster simplification, from Sanskrit रण्ड (raṇḍa). Related to رَانْڈ (rānḍ, “widow, prostitute”).

senses.g: the act of whining or constantly complaining; bitching and moaning

رنگ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀭𑀁𑀕 (raṃga), from Sanskrit रङ्ग (raṅga), which is derived from the root रञ्ज् (rañj). Also influenced by Classical Persian رنگ (rang). In either case, ultimately from Proto-Indo-Iranian *(H)rag-, *(H)ranǰ- (“to dye, color”), of unclear origin. Cognate with Gujarati રંગ (raṅg), Marathi रंग (raṅga), Punjabi رَن٘گ (raṉg) and Odia ରଙ୍ଗ (raṅga).

senses.g: Rangpur (a city, a divisional capital in Rangpur Division, Bangladesh)
a rangoli (painted decoration on the floor)

رنگولی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀭𑀁𑀕𑀉𑀮𑀺 (*raṃgaüli), from Sanskrit रङ्गावलि (raṅgāvali).

رنگین کمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَنْگِینکَمَان (rangīn-kamān), from رنگین (rangīn /rangin) + کمان (kamān /kamân).

روان.e: From Classical Persian روان (ravān, “fluid, flowing”), a compound of رو (rav) + ـان (-ân); the former is the present stem of رفتن (raftan).

روانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روانه (rawāna), from رو (raw), the present stem of رفتن (raftan, “to go, leave”). Compare Punjabi ਰਵਾਨਾ (ravānā) / روانہ (ravānā), Bengali রওয়ানা (roōẇana), Marathi रवाना (ravānā).

Borrowed from Classical Persian روانه (rawāna), from رو (raw), the present stem of رفتن (raftan, “to go, leave”). Compare Punjabi ਰਵਾਨਾ (ravānā) / روانہ (ravānā), Bengali রওয়ানা (roōẇana), Marathi रवाना (ravānā).

روانئ خون.e: From روانی (ravānī, “fluid, flowing”) + ئ (-e, “ezafe”) + خون (xūn, “blood”), literally meaning "flow of blood", either borrowed through Classical Persian or a Persianized Urdu-borne formation.

senses.g: en route, set off (of a vehicle, group etc. on a journey)
free (ie. of part of a machine)

روایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رِوایَت (riwāyat), from Arabic رِوَايَة (riwāya).

senses.g: outward appearance, form, figure, shape, appearance, semblance, image, representation, picture
handsome form or figure, shapeliness, beauty, elegance, grace
discrete quantity, the number one, arithmetical unit; integer number
the known or absolute number, known quantity
a foreign currency of some kind.
rupees, inflection of رُوپَیَہ (rūpaya, “rupee”): — oblique/vocative singular
rupees, inflection of رُوپَیَہ (rūpaya, “rupee”): — direct plural

روٹی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit रोट्ट- (rŏṭṭa-), from Sanskrit रोटिका (roṭikā, “a kind of bread”).

ex.e: don't be angry

روٹھنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀭𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀞 (ruṭṭha, “angry”) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix), the first element of which is from Sanskrit रुष्ट (ruṣṭa).

senses.g: Rochdale (a town and metropolitan borough of Greater Manchester, England)

روچڈیل.e: Borrowed from English Rochdale. Likely influenced by Pakistani English pronunciation.

روح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روح (rūḥ), from Arabic رُوح (rūḥ, “spirit”).

senses.g: Rooh Afza (brand of concentrated syrup generally mixed with either water or milk to create a squash)

روح افزا.e: Coined by Naqi Ali Shah in 1906.

روح القدس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روحالقدس (rūhu-l-quds), from Arabic رُوحُ القُدُس (rūḥu l-qudus).

روحانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روحانی (rūhānī), from Arabic رُوحَانِيّ (rūḥāniyy).

رود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُود (rūd), from Middle Persian lwd (/⁠rōd⁠/, “river”), from Old Persian 𐎼𐎢𐎫 (r-u-t /⁠rautaʰ⁠⁠/, “river, torrent”), from Proto-Iranian *hráwtah, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *sráwtas, from Proto-Indo-European *srew-, possibly from *ser-.
